skills.aiInstallPrompt:
Install the skill 'annual-appeal-email' by writing the file below.

Universal path: ./skills/annual-appeal-email/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/annual-appeal-email/SKILL.md
Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/annual-appeal-email/SKILL.md
Claude path: ~/.claude/skills/annual-appeal-email/SKILL.md
VS Code agent path: .github/skills/annual-appeal-email/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: annual-appeal-email
description: >
  Writes an annual appeal email/letter segmented by donor tier (lapsed, first-time, recurring, past-major) with the ask and story matched to each segment instead of one blast to the whole list. Triggers: "write our year-end annual appeal email", "help me draft the annual fund letter for all our donor segments", /annual-appeal-email.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /annual-appeal-email

Writes a segmented annual appeal - distinct versions for lapsed donors, first-time donors, recurring donors, and past major donors - each with a story and ask matched to that segment's actual relationship with the organization.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The organization is preparing its annual or year-end fundraising appeal and wants it segmented by donor relationship rather than one identical email blasted to the entire list - most valuable because a lapsed donor, a first-timer, and a loyal recurring donor each need a different message to actually respond.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific need or story this year's appeal is built around (a program, a matching gift opportunity, a funding gap) - concrete, not generic year-end language
- Rough donor segments available (lapsed, first-time/new, recurring/monthly, past major donors) and roughly how many are in each, if known
- Whether there's a matching gift or deadline element this year (creates real, not manufactured, urgency)
- Suggested ask amounts by segment, if the organization has giving history data to base them on

## Safety

- Any tax-deductibility language should reflect the organization's current, accurate tax-exempt status - confirm this wording with the organization's finance/legal function before sending.
- Propose before acting: share all segment drafts for review; wait for explicit user confirmation before any version is sent.

## Workflow

1. Write the lapsed-donor version to explicitly acknowledge the gap without guilt-tripping - reference (generally, not awkwardly specific) that it's been a while, reintroduce what's changed or grown at the organization, and make it easy to restart giving with a low-friction ask, since shame-based language reliably backfires and suppresses response.
2. Write the first-time/new-donor version to build the relationship, not just repeat the ask - include a welcome/orientation note about the organization's work alongside the appeal, since a brand-new donor doesn't yet have the context a recurring donor has.
3. Write the recurring-donor version to acknowledge their consistency directly and specifically ("your monthly gift has funded X this year") and frame the ask as an addition or upgrade opportunity, not a repeat of the same ask they already fulfill automatically.
4. Write the past-major-donor version with more personal framing and a specific suggested amount tied to their prior giving level, rather than the same broad ask used for smaller general-list segments - this segment usually deserves a version that reads like it was written with them specifically in mind.
5. Build the shared story/need section once (the concrete program or funding gap this appeal centers on) then adapt the framing and ask per segment, so the core narrative stays consistent across segments while the relationship-specific framing differs.
6. Include a real, honest urgency element if one exists (a matching gift deadline, a fiscal year-end need) - but do not manufacture urgency that isn't genuine, since donors increasingly recognize and discount fake scarcity language.

## Output format

```
# Annual Appeal - [Year] - Core story: [program/need this appeal centers on]

## Segment 1: Lapsed donors
Subject: [subject]
[Body - acknowledges gap without guilt, reintroduces org, low-friction ask]

## Segment 2: First-time/new donors
Subject: [subject]
[Body - welcome/context + ask]

## Segment 3: Recurring donors
Subject: [subject]
[Body - acknowledges consistency specifically, frames ask as addition/upgrade]

## Segment 4: Past major donors
Subject: [subject]
[Body - personal framing, specific suggested amount tied to prior giving level]

## Shared elements across all segments
Core story/need: [consistent narrative]
Real urgency element (if any): [matching gift/deadline - confirm it's genuine]
```

## Do
- Write a genuinely distinct version for each donor segment, not one email with a greeting swap
- Reference recurring donors' specific giving pattern and past major donors' specific prior level
- Use only real urgency (a genuine deadline or match), never manufactured scarcity

## Don't
- Don't guilt-trip lapsed donors about the gap in giving
- Don't send new donors the same "thank you for your continued support" language meant for repeat donors
- Don't invent a fake deadline or matching gift that doesn't actually exist

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming tax-exempt/deductibility language with finance/legal
- [ ] All four segment versions are meaningfully different, not interchangeable with a name swap
- [ ] Any urgency/deadline language used is confirmed genuine, not manufactured
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /annual-appeal-email or describe the task from the skill description.

Universal path: ./skills/donor-thank-you-sequence/SKILL.md
Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/donor-thank-you-sequence/SKILL.md
Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/donor-thank-you-sequence/SKILL.md
Claude path: ~/.claude/skills/donor-thank-you-sequence/SKILL.md
VS Code agent path: .github/skills/donor-thank-you-sequence/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: donor-thank-you-sequence
description: >
  Writes a multi-touch donor thank-you sequence (immediate receipt-style acknowledgment, personal follow-up, and impact update) calibrated to gift size and donor history, not one generic thank-you email. Triggers: "write a thank-you email for a donor who just gave $500", "help me build a thank-you sequence for our year-end donors", /donor-thank-you-sequence.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /donor-thank-you-sequence

Writes a multi-touch donor thank-you sequence - immediate acknowledgment, personal follow-up, and later impact update - calibrated to this donor's actual gift size and giving history, not one generic template reused for every gift.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A gift just came in - whether a small first-time online donation or a larger gift from a known donor - and staff want a thank-you sequence that matches the relationship, not a single form letter sent identically to a $25 first-timer and a $5,000 repeat donor.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Gift amount and whether it's a first-time or repeat gift
- Donor's giving history if known (past gifts, whether they volunteer too, any personal connection to the mission)
- What the gift will actually fund, specifically, if known (a program, a general fund, a campaign) so impact language is concrete rather than vague
- Whether a phone call or handwritten note is realistic for this gift size (changes what the "personal follow-up" touch should be)

## Safety

- This is draft communication for the organization's own review before sending - confirm tax-receipt language meets current requirements for charitable donation acknowledgments in the organization's jurisdiction (e.g. required statements about goods/services received) with the organization's finance or legal function before using it as an official receipt.
- Propose before acting: show the full sequence; wait for explicit user confirmation before anything is sent to the donor.

## Workflow

1. Write the immediate acknowledgment (sent within 24-48 hours) as a warm but efficient message that also functions as the tax-receipt touchpoint - include the required elements of a donation acknowledgment (amount, date, organization's tax-exempt status statement, and a statement about goods/services received in exchange, if any) since this email often needs to double as the donor's tax record.
2. Scale the personal follow-up touch to gift size: a modest first-time gift might get a warm templated-but-personalized email; a significant gift or a known long-time donor deserves a genuinely personal note referencing their specific history with the organization, and if realistic for the org's capacity, a phone call or handwritten note rather than only email.
3. Make the impact language in every touch as concrete as possible - tie the gift to an actual outcome or program ("your gift helps cover a week of the after-school tutoring program") rather than generic "you're making a difference" language that could apply to any cause.
4. For repeat or long-time donors, explicitly acknowledge the pattern of their giving ("this is your fourth gift this year" or "you've supported us since [year]") since recognizing loyalty is one of the most effective and most often skipped parts of donor stewardship.
5. Schedule a later impact-update touch (weeks to months out, not immediate) that closes the loop on what the gift specifically enabled, so the donor sees the outcome of their generosity, not just a thank-you and silence until the next ask.
6. Keep every touch focused on gratitude and impact only - no fundraising ask should appear in a thank-you sequence; mixing a thank-you with a new ask undermines the sincerity of both.

## Output format

```
# Donor Thank-You Sequence - [Donor Name], Gift: $[amount], [first-time/repeat]

## Touch 1: Immediate acknowledgment (within 24-48 hours)
Subject: [subject]
[Body - includes tax-receipt elements: amount, date, org tax-exempt statement, goods/services statement]

## Touch 2: Personal follow-up ([email/call/handwritten note - matched to gift size])
[Script or note content, referencing donor's specific history if known]

## Touch 3: Impact update (send [X weeks/months] later)
[Body - concrete outcome tied to what this gift specifically funded]

## Notes for staff
Suggested channel for Touch 2: [email/call/note]
Recognition detail used: [donor's specific giving history referenced]
```

## Do
- Include required tax-receipt elements in the immediate acknowledgment
- Scale the personal-touch channel (email vs. call vs. note) to gift size and donor history
- Keep every touch free of any new fundraising ask

## Don't
- Don't send an identical generic thank-you regardless of gift size or donor history
- Don't include a donation ask anywhere in a thank-you sequence
- Don't use vague "making a difference" language when a concrete program/outcome tie-in is available

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming tax-receipt language with finance/legal before official use
- [ ] Approval gate present before anything is sent to the donor
- [ ] No fundraising ask appears anywhere in the sequence
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /donor-thank-you-sequence or describe the task from the skill description.


## Do

- Don't include a donation ask anywhere in a thank-you sequence
- Don't use vague "making a difference" language when a concrete program/outcome tie-in is available

- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready

- [ ] Approval gate present before anything is sent to the donor
- [ ] No fundraising ask appears anywhere in the sequence

Universal path: ./skills/impact-report-summary/SKILL.md

Cursor path: ~/.cursor/skills/impact-report-summary/SKILL.md

VS Code agent path: .github/skills/impact-report-summary/SKILL.md

Create the folder if needed, then write SKILL.md with this exact content:

```markdown
---
name: impact-report-summary
description: >
  Turns raw program data (numbers served, outcomes, budget usage) into a donor/board-facing impact report that leads with outcomes over outputs and is honest about what the data can and can't prove. Triggers: "turn our program numbers into an impact report for donors", "help me write our annual impact summary", /impact-report-summary.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: medium
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /impact-report-summary

Turns raw program data into a donor/board-facing impact report that leads with real outcomes rather than just activity counts, and is explicit about what the data actually demonstrates versus what it merely suggests.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A nonprofit has program data (people served, sessions run, outcomes tracked, budget spent) from a period (quarter, year, campaign) and needs to turn it into a report for donors, a board, or a grant funder that tells a real story rather than a wall of disconnected statistics.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Raw numbers available: people/households served, sessions/units of service delivered, any outcome data actually measured (not just activity counts)
- Time period covered and how it compares to the prior period, if known
- Audience for this report (individual donors, board, a specific grant funder - framing and required detail differ)
- Any specific story, quote, or case example available to illustrate the numbers (with appropriate privacy/consent already handled by the org)

## Safety

- Only use client/beneficiary stories or identifying details that the organization has confirmed proper consent to share publicly - flag this explicitly if it's unclear, and default to anonymized/composite framing when consent status is unknown.
- Do not inflate, extrapolate, or imply outcomes the underlying data doesn't actually support - if the org only has output data (activities delivered) and not outcome data (change achieved), say so honestly rather than implying causation the data can't prove.
- Propose before acting: share the draft report for the user's review; wait for explicit confirmation before it's distributed to donors or published.

## Workflow

1. Distinguish outputs (what the org did - meals served, sessions held, people enrolled) from outcomes (what changed as a result - skills gained, stability achieved, measured improvement) and lead the report with outcomes wherever real outcome data exists, since donors respond to change achieved, not just activity volume.
2. If only output data is available (common for smaller orgs without formal outcome measurement), say so plainly rather than implying outcomes the org can't actually demonstrate - e.g. "we don't yet formally track long-term outcomes, but here's what we delivered this year" is more credible than vague causal language that overstates what's known.
3. Contextualize every number with a comparison point where possible - prior year, a stated goal, or a rate/ratio (e.g. "cost per person served") - since a bare number ("we served 3,200 people") means little without something to compare it against.
4. Weave in one concrete, real story or case example (properly consented) to make the aggregate numbers feel human, but clearly label it as an illustrative example, not a claim that it represents the average outcome.
5. Tie spending/budget data to outcomes transparently if included - donors and funders increasingly want to see efficiency context (e.g. cost per outcome), and hiding or omitting this can read as evasive even when the org's actual efficiency is reasonable.
6. Close with what's next - a stated goal or focus for the coming period - so the report ends by inviting continued partnership, not just closing the book on the past period.

## Output format

```
# Impact Report - [Period] - [Organization/Program Name]
Audience: [donors/board/funder]

## The headline outcome (or output, if outcome data isn't available)
[1-2 sentence real, specific result - honestly framed as outcome or output]

## By the numbers
| Metric | This period | Prior period (if known) | Context/ratio |
|---|---|---|---|

## What changed (outcomes) vs. what we did (outputs)
Outcomes we can demonstrate: [list, or "not yet formally tracked"]
Outputs delivered: [list]

## A story behind the numbers
[Real, consented example - labeled as illustrative, not representative of average]

## Budget/efficiency context (if included)
[Cost per outcome/output, framed transparently]

## What's next
[Stated goal or focus for the coming period]
```

## Do
- Lead with real outcomes when outcome data exists; be honest when it doesn't
- Contextualize every number with a comparison point
- Label illustrative stories clearly as examples, not statistical claims

## Don't
- Don't imply causal outcomes from activity/output data alone
- Don't use a beneficiary story without confirmed consent - default to anonymized framing if unsure
- Don't present raw numbers with no comparison point or context

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer/consent check present regarding any beneficiary story used
- [ ] Report is honest about outputs vs. outcomes, not overstating what the data proves
- [ ] Every key number has a comparison point or context, not presented in isolation
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /impact-report-summary or describe the task from the skill description.

1. Distinguish outputs (what the org did - meals served, sessions held, people enrolled) from outcomes (what changed as a result - skills gained, stability achieved, measured improvement) and lead the report with outcomes wherever real outcome data exists, since donors respond to change achieved, not just activity volume.
2. If only output data is available (common for smaller orgs without formal outcome measurement), say so plainly rather than implying outcomes the org can't actually demonstrate - e.g. "we don't yet formally track long-term outcomes, but here's what we delivered this year" is more credible than vague causal language that overstates what's known.
3. Contextualize every number with a comparison point where possible - prior year, a stated goal, or a rate/ratio (e.g. "cost per person served") - since a bare number ("we served 3,200 people") means little without something to compare it against.
4. Weave in one concrete, real story or case example (properly consented) to make the aggregate numbers feel human, but clearly label it as an illustrative example, not a claim that it represents the average outcome.
5. Tie spending/budget data to outcomes transparently if included - donors and funders increasingly want to see efficiency context (e.g. cost per outcome), and hiding or omitting this can read as evasive even when the org's actual efficiency is reasonable.
6. Close with what's next - a stated goal or focus for the coming period - so the report ends by inviting continued partnership, not just closing the book on the past period.

## Output format

```
# Impact Report - [Period] - [Organization/Program Name]
Audience: [donors/board/funder]

## The headline outcome (or output, if outcome data isn't available)
[1-2 sentence real, specific result - honestly framed as outcome or output]

## By the numbers
| Metric | This period | Prior period (if known) | Context/ratio |
|---|---|---|---|

## What changed (outcomes) vs. what we did (outputs)
Outcomes we can demonstrate: [list, or "not yet formally tracked"]
Outputs delivered: [list]

## A story behind the numbers
[Real, consented example - labeled as illustrative, not representative of average]

## Budget/efficiency context (if included)
[Cost per outcome/output, framed transparently]

## What's next
[Stated goal or focus for the coming period]
```

## Do
- Lead with real outcomes when outcome data exists; be honest when it doesn't
- Contextualize every number with a comparison point
- Label illustrative stories clearly as examples, not statistical claims

## Don't
- Don't imply causal outcomes from activity/output data alone
- Don't use a beneficiary story without confirmed consent - default to anonymized framing if unsure
- Don't present raw numbers with no comparison point or context

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer/consent check present regarding any beneficiary story used
- [ ] Report is honest about outputs vs. outcomes, not overstating what the data proves
- [ ] Every key number has a comparison point or context, not presented in isolation

```markdown
---
name: major-gift-ask-letter
description: >
  Drafts a major-gift solicitation letter (or talking points for an in-person ask) built around this specific donor's relationship history and a named gift amount, not a generic appeal letter with a number filled in. Triggers: "help me write a major gift ask letter for a longtime donor", "draft talking points for asking a donor for a specific gift amount", /major-gift-ask-letter.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: high
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /major-gift-ask-letter

Drafts a major-gift solicitation letter or in-person ask talking points built around this specific donor's actual relationship history, giving pattern, and connection to the mission, with a named specific gift amount rather than a generic appeal.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A development officer or executive director is preparing to make (or write) a significant ask to a known donor - someone with real capacity and an established relationship with the organization - where a generic annual-appeal letter would badly undersell the moment and the relationship.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The donor's giving history and relationship with the organization (years giving, past gift levels, any personal connection - board service, volunteer history, a personal story about why they care)
- The specific gift amount being asked for and what it would fund (a named project, an endowment, general operating - specificity matters)
- Who is making the ask (a staff member, a board member, a peer donor) and in what format (letter, in-person meeting, phone call)
- Any known motivation or values the donor has expressed previously (what they've said matters to them about the mission)

## Safety

- This is a draft for the organization's own review, not a guarantee of tax-deductibility advice for the donor - any specific tax-benefit claims about the gift should be reviewed by the organization's finance/legal function, and donors should be encouraged to confirm specifics with their own tax advisor.
- Propose before acting: share the draft letter or talking points; wait for explicit user confirmation before anything is sent or the ask is made.
- Respect donor dignity and autonomy in the language - never use pressure tactics, guilt, or artificial urgency that could feel coercive to a major donor.

## Workflow

1. Open by naming the donor's actual relationship history specifically - years of giving, a past gift's impact, board or volunteer involvement, or a personal reason they've shared for caring about the cause - since a major-gift ask that doesn't demonstrate the org actually knows this donor personally undermines the entire premise of a "major gift" relationship.
2. State a specific, named gift amount and a specific, named use for it - vague asks ("would you consider a generous gift") perform worse with donors who have the capacity and relationship for a real, concrete ask; naming both the number and the exact use signals the organization has thought carefully about this donor's fit for this specific need.
3. Frame the ask around the donor's own expressed values and interests wherever known, not a generic case for the whole organization - if this donor has previously said they care specifically about a program area, lead with that connection rather than a broad institutional pitch.
4. Include the "why now" reasoning honestly - a real capital need, a matching gift opportunity, a specific funding gap - but avoid manufactured urgency or pressure language that doesn't reflect the actual situation.
5. If this is for an in-person or phone ask, build talking points that include how to actually say the number out loud (many asks fail because the solicitor never states a specific figure) and how to sit with silence after asking, rather than filling the pause nervously.
6. Close with genuine respect for the donor's decision-making process - an explicit statement that whatever they decide, the relationship and gratitude for past support stand, since preserving the long-term relationship matters more than any single ask.

## Output format

```
# Major Gift Ask - [Donor Name]
Format: [letter / in-person talking points / phone call]
Ask amount: $[X] | Designated for: [specific project/use]

## Donor relationship context (used to personalize the ask)
[Giving history, personal connection, expressed values]

## Draft letter (if applicable)
[Full letter text: personal opening referencing relationship, case for this specific gift,
named amount and use, why now, respectful close]

## Talking points (if in-person/phone ask)
Opening: [reference to relationship]
The ask, stated directly: "[exact scripted line stating the specific number]"
After the ask: [reminder to pause, let silence sit]
If they ask questions: [anticipated Qs and answers]
Closing regardless of outcome: [respectful, relationship-preserving close]
```

## Do
- Name a specific gift amount and specific use - avoid vague "generous gift" language
- Open with real, specific relationship history, not a generic donor greeting
- Include guidance to state the number directly and sit with silence afterward

## Don't
- Don't use guilt, pressure, or manufactured urgency
- Don't send a generic appeal-letter version of this to a donor with real major-gift capacity and history
- Don't skip stating the actual number - vague asks underperform specific ones

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present about confirming tax-benefit claims with finance/legal and encouraging donor to consult their own tax advisor
- [ ] Approval gate present before anything is sent or the ask is made
- [ ] A specific gift amount and specific designated use are both named, not left vague
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /major-gift-ask-letter or describe the task from the skill description.

```markdown
---
name: volunteer-appreciation-event-invite
description: >
  Writes a volunteer appreciation event invitation that names specific volunteer contributions and impact rather than a generic "thank you for your service" mass invite, plus a short recognition-moment script for the event itself. Triggers: "write an invite for our volunteer appreciation dinner", "help me plan what to say to recognize volunteers at our event", /volunteer-appreciation-event-invite.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand

# /volunteer-appreciation-event-invite

Writes a volunteer appreciation event invitation grounded in specific, real volunteer contributions from the period being celebrated, plus a short recognition-moment script for use live at the event.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The organization is hosting a volunteer appreciation event (a dinner, a picnic, an awards ceremony, even an informal gathering) and needs both the invitation copy and a plan for how to actually recognize people meaningfully once they're there, rather than a generic "thanks for all you do" that could apply to any organization's volunteers.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Event details: date, time, location, format (dinner, picnic, awards ceremony)
- The specific period being celebrated and 2-3 concrete collective accomplishments volunteers achieved (hours served, a milestone reached, a program made possible)
- Any individual volunteers being specially recognized at the event, and what specifically they did
- Tone the organization wants (formal awards ceremony vs. casual thank-you gathering)

## Workflow

1. Open the invitation with a specific, real accomplishment from the volunteer group, not a generic sentiment - "because of your [X hours / specific project], we were able to [specific outcome]" lands as genuine recognition in a way "thank you for your service" alone does not.
2. State event logistics clearly and early (date, time, location, RSVP method) since a heartfelt invite that's confusing about basic logistics undermines its own purpose.
3. If specific individuals will be recognized at the event, prepare short, specific recognition notes for each - naming exactly what they did, not a generic "outstanding volunteer" label - since specific recognition is what people actually remember and value.
4. Prepare a recognition-moment script for the event itself (a toast, a short speech, an awards-presentation script) that opens with the group's collective impact before narrowing to any individual shout-outs, so everyone in the room feels the moment is about them collectively, not just the few being individually named.
5. Include a moment in the script that invites volunteers to hear directly (a video message, a written quote, a live guest) from someone the organization serves, when feasible and consented - hearing directly from a beneficiary is often the most moving recognition moment an organization can offer volunteers.
6. Close the invite and the event script with a forward-looking note (what's coming next, how volunteers can stay involved) so appreciation connects to continued engagement rather than feeling like a one-time closing ceremony.

## Output format

```
## Event invitation
Subject: [subject line]

[Invite body: specific accomplishment opener, event logistics, warm close, RSVP instructions]

---
## Event recognition script

Opening (collective impact): [script]

[If individual recognitions:]
Individual shout-outs:
- [Volunteer name]: [specific contribution recognized]
- [Volunteer name]: [specific contribution recognized]

[If feasible: beneficiary voice moment - video/quote/guest]: [note/script]

Closing (forward-looking): [script]
```

## Do
- Open with a specific, real, collective accomplishment, not generic gratitude language
- Prepare individually specific recognition notes for any named shout-outs
- Include a beneficiary-voice moment when feasible and properly consented

## Don't
- Don't use generic "outstanding volunteer" labels without naming the specific contribution
- Don't bury event logistics under sentiment - make date/time/RSVP clear and early
- Don't end on pure nostalgia - close with how volunteers can stay engaged going forward

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Invitation opens with a specific, real accomplishment, not generic thank-you language
- [ ] Event logistics (date/time/location/RSVP) are clear and easy to find
- [ ] Any individual recognition is specific to that person's actual contribution
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /volunteer-appreciation-event-invite or describe the task from the skill description.

Grok path: ~/.grok/skills/volunteer-recruitment-post/SKILL.md

```markdown
---
name: volunteer-recruitment-post
description: >
  Writes a volunteer recruitment post/listing that leads with a specific, time-bound task and real impact statement rather than a vague "we need help" appeal, tuned to the platform it will run on. Triggers: "write a volunteer recruitment post for our food bank shift", "help me recruit volunteers for our weekend event", /volunteer-recruitment-post.
metadata:
  category: nonprofit-donor-volunteer-engagement
  type: base
  cadence: on-demand
  risk: low
allowed-tools: search_tool, use_tool
---
# /volunteer-recruitment-post

Writes a volunteer recruitment post that leads with a specific, time-bound task and a concrete impact statement, tuned to the platform it will run on (social post, community board, volunteer-matching site, email blast).

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A nonprofit has a specific volunteer need - a one-time event, an ongoing weekly shift, a skills-based project - and needs a recruitment post that gets someone to actually sign up, not a generic "we always need volunteers" appeal that reads as background noise.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific role/task, date(s)/time commitment, and location (in-person or remote)
- What skills or qualifications are actually required vs. none needed (be honest - overstating requirements shrinks the volunteer pool the same way it shrinks a job applicant pool)
- The concrete impact of this specific role (what happens because this task gets done - not the org's whole mission restated)
- Platform this will run on (Facebook community group, Nextdoor, a volunteer-matching platform, an email newsletter, Instagram) since format and tone differ

## Workflow

1. Lead with the specific task and time commitment in the first line, not the organization's broader mission statement - "we need 4 people this Saturday 9am-12pm to sort donated clothing" gets more signups than "join us in our mission to fight poverty," because specificity lowers the mental friction of deciding to say yes.
2. State the real time commitment honestly and precisely (exact hours, one-time vs. recurring, any training required) - vague asks ("just a few hours whenever you can") create hesitation because people can't picture what they're actually committing to.
3. Tie the task to a concrete outcome, not the org's whole mission - explain what specifically happens because of this shift or project (e.g. "this batch of sorted clothing goes directly to families displaced by [specific recent event]") since concrete impact recruits better than abstract cause language.
4. List only genuinely required qualifications (age minimums for legal/safety reasons, specific certifications if truly needed) and explicitly say "no experience necessary" if that's true - this is the single biggest lever for expanding the volunteer pool, the same way overstated job requirements shrink an applicant pool.
5. Make the sign-up action as low-friction as the platform allows (a single link, a simple reply, a direct contact) and state it clearly at the end, not buried in a paragraph.
6. Match tone and length to the platform: a community Facebook group post can be warm and slightly longer; a volunteer-matching platform listing should be scannable with clear structured fields; an Instagram caption needs a strong opening line since captions truncate.

## Output format

```
# Volunteer Recruitment Post - [Task/Event] - Platform: [platform]

[Post text, structured for the platform:]
- Specific task + time commitment (first line)
- Concrete impact tie-in
- Requirements (only real ones, or "no experience necessary")
- Clear sign-up action

---
## Quick-reference fields (for volunteer-matching platform listings, if applicable)
Role: [title]
Date/time: [specifics]
Location: [in-person address / remote]
Requirements: [list or "none"]
Sign-up: [link/contact]
```

## Do
- Lead with the specific task and exact time commitment, not the mission statement
- State the concrete outcome this specific role produces
- Say "no experience necessary" explicitly when true

## Don't
- Don't open with abstract mission language before the actual ask
- Don't overstate requirements that would exclude willing, capable volunteers
- Don't leave the time commitment vague ("just a few hours sometime")

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] First line states the specific task and time commitment, not general mission talk
- [ ] Impact statement is concrete and tied to this specific task
- [ ] Sign-up action is a single, low-friction, clearly stated step
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /volunteer-recruitment-post or describe the task from the skill description.

Writes a volunteer recruitment post that leads with a specific, time-bound task and a concrete impact statement, tuned to the platform it will run on (social post, community board, volunteer-matching site, email blast).

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A nonprofit has a specific volunteer need - a one-time event, an ongoing weekly shift, a skills-based project - and needs a recruitment post that gets someone to actually sign up, not a generic "we always need volunteers" appeal that reads as background noise.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific role/task, date(s)/time commitment, and location (in-person or remote)
- What skills or qualifications are actually required vs. none needed (be honest - overstating requirements shrinks the volunteer pool the same way it shrinks a job applicant pool)
- The concrete impact of this specific role (what happens because this task gets done - not the org's whole mission restated)
- Platform this will run on (Facebook community group, Nextdoor, a volunteer-matching platform, an email newsletter, Instagram) since format and tone differ

## Workflow

1. Lead with the specific task and time commitment in the first line, not the organization's broader mission statement - "we need 4 people this Saturday 9am-12pm to sort donated clothing" gets more signups than "join us in our mission to fight poverty," because specificity lowers the mental friction of deciding to say yes.
2. State the real time commitment honestly and precisely (exact hours, one-time vs. recurring, any training required) - vague asks ("just a few hours whenever you can") create hesitation because people can't picture what they're actually committing to.
3. Tie the task to a concrete outcome, not the org's whole mission - explain what specifically happens because of this shift or project (e.g. "this batch of sorted clothing goes directly to families displaced by [specific recent event]") since concrete impact recruits better than abstract cause language.
4. List only genuinely required qualifications (age minimums for legal/safety reasons, specific certifications if truly needed) and explicitly say "no experience necessary" if that's true - this is the single biggest lever for expanding the volunteer pool, the same way overstated job requirements shrink an applicant pool.
5. Make the sign-up action as low-friction as the platform allows (a single link, a simple reply, a direct contact) and state it clearly at the end, not buried in a paragraph.
6. Match tone and length to the platform: a community Facebook group post can be warm and slightly longer; a volunteer-matching platform listing should be scannable with clear structured fields; an Instagram caption needs a strong opening line since captions truncate.

## Output format

```
# Volunteer Recruitment Post - [Task/Event] - Platform: [platform]

[Post text, structured for the platform:]
- Specific task + time commitment (first line)
- Concrete impact tie-in
- Requirements (only real ones, or "no experience necessary")
- Clear sign-up action

---
## Quick-reference fields (for volunteer-matching platform listings, if applicable)
Role: [title]
Date/time: [specifics]
Location: [in-person address / remote]
Requirements: [list or "none"]
Sign-up: [link/contact]
```

## Do
- Lead with the specific task and exact time commitment, not the mission statement
- State the concrete outcome this specific role produces
- Say "no experience necessary" explicitly when true

## Don't
- Don't open with abstract mission language before the actual ask
- Don't overstate requirements that would exclude willing, capable volunteers
- Don't leave the time commitment vague ("just a few hours sometime")

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] First line states the specific task and time commitment, not general mission talk
- [ ] Impact statement is concrete and tied to this specific task
- [ ] Sign-up action is a single, low-friction, clearly stated step
